title: Alum pre-mordant for wool
subtitle: Preparing wool fibres to accept and hold natural dye
excerpt: Mordanting bonds a metal salt to the fibre before dyeing so that the dye molecules have something to grip. Alum (potassium aluminium sulphate) is the standard first mordant for wool: it is relatively safe, brightens most colours, and does not significantly alter the hand of the fibre.
sourceNotes: Alum mordanting of wool is described in natural dyeing texts including Jenny Dean's 'Wild Colour' and Gill Dalby's 'Natural Dyes for Vegetable Fibres'. The 10-15% WOF standard is consistent across sources. Observation-led.

content.text: is a metal salt that bonds to the fibre and gives dye molecules a surface to grip. Wool mordanted with alum gives clear, bright colours with most plant dyes. The standard dose is 10-15%
(10-15 g alum per 100 g of dry fibre). Higher doses do not improve colour uptake and can make the fibre feel sticky.

attrs.body: Wear dedicated gloves, work in a ventilated space, and keep mordant jars away from food and children.
attrs.title: Mordant safety

items.name: Washed, wetted wool fleece or yarn
Cream of tartar (optional, brightens and softens fibre)
Stainless steel dye pot, minimum 4 litre
Dedicated wooden spoon or dowel for stirring

content.text: Weigh the dry fibre, then soak it in warm water for at least 20 minutes so it is wet through. Dissolve the alum (and cream of tartar if using) in a cup of hot water, stirring until fully clear. Pour the dissolved alum into the dye pot filled with enough water to let the fibre move freely.
Step 2: Enter the fibre and heat slowly

Gently lower the wetted fibre into the mordant bath. Heat the pot slowly over 30-40 minutes to 80-85°C, stirring gently every few minutes. Do not boil wool: boiling causes felting and damages the fibre. Maintain 80-85°C for 45-60 minutes.

Allow the pot to cool to 40°C before removing the fibre. Lift the fibre gently without wringing or agitating. Rinse in water at the same temperature as the pot to avoid thermal shock, which can cause felting. The mordanted fibre can be dyed immediately while still wet, or dried and stored for later use.

still contains residual alum after the first batch. It can be used for one or two further small batches at the same temperature. After three batches, the alum concentration is too low to be effective. Dispose of the cooled bath by diluting heavily with water before pouring slowly into a drain (not a garden, as aluminium salts can affect soil pH).

items.fix: There is no way to remove excess alum from the fibre. The stickiness usually diminishes after dyeing and rinsing, but may persist. Use no more than 15% WOF alum in future.
items.cause: Too much alum was used (above 20% WOF).

items.fix: Pre-wet fibre for longer and heat the mordant bath very slowly. Stir gently during the mordant stage to move all parts of the fibre through the bath.
items.cause: The mordant did not enter the fibre evenly, either because the heating was too fast or the fibre was not thoroughly pre-wetted.
items.symptom: Colour from the dye bath is patchy or uneven
